request.question: Which town was the director of the "Red Curtain Trilogy" born in?

title: Red Curtain Trilogy
Baz Luhrmann
Michael O'Hare (chef)
Baby, Baby, Baby (Teresa Brewer song)
Insomnia (band)
Strictly Ballroom
Miss Utah Teen USA
Nina Ananiashvili
Kambuzia Partovi
Herons Creek, New South Wales
sentences: The Red Curtain Trilogy is the title given to the first three films directed by Baz Luhrmann:

Mark Anthony "Baz" Luhrmann ( ; born 17 September 1962) is an Australian film director, screenwriter and producer best known for "Red Curtain Trilogy", comprising his romantic comedy film "Strictly Ballroom" (1992), the romantic drama "Romeo + Juliet" (1996), and the pastiche-jukebox musical "Moulin Rouge!
His 2008 film "Australia" is an epic historical romantic drama film starring Hugh Jackman and Nicole Kidman.
His 2013 drama "The Great Gatsby", based on F. Scott Fitzgerald's 1925 novel of the same name, stars Leonardo DiCaprio (whom he also used in "Romeo + Juliet") and Tobey Maguire.
Michael O'Hare (born June 1981) is a British chef from Redcar, North Yorkshire, England.
He is chef-patron at The Man Behind The Curtain in Leeds, which was awarded a Michelin star in October 2015.
He is also Creative Director of GG Hospitality and oversees the company's restaurant The Rabbit in the Moon at the National Football Museum in Manchester.
Baby, Baby, Baby is a 1953 hit song by Teresa Brewer from the film "Those Redheads from Seattle".
The song was written in 1950 by Jerry Livingston and lyricist Mack David.

The song was sung by Brewer in the role of a singer, who appears through a red curtain line of dancing girls and commences the lyrics: "Baby, Baby, Baby love me love me do, Baby, Baby, Baby love me love true."
The song reached No.12 in the US hit parade in December 1953.

Coral released the record first in 1953 as 9-61067 with the B-side "I Guess It Was You All The Time", written by Hoagy Carmichael and Johnny Mercer, then in 1954 as an EP, EC 81086 with A2: "Jilted" Robert Colby and Dick Manning, Track B1: "Chicago Style" James Van Heusen and Johnny Burke, and B2 "My Sweetie Went Away (She Didn't Say Where, When Or Why)" written by Rey Turk, and Lou Handman.
Insomnia (Bengali: ইনসমনিয়া ) was a rock band based in Kolkata, West Bengal.
The band's repertoire included both English and Bengali songs.
The band has an album each in English ("Cry of the Spirit" - self released) and Bengali ("Proloyer Shomoye" - Asha Audio).

Insomnia were also quite adept at executing live music for theatre, being part of the act itself, in plays such as "Peacewards" (directed by Jayant Kripalani, produced by Red Curtain Productions, based on monologues written by Manjula Padmanabhan), a 3rd theatre version of "Rokto Korobi" (written by Rabindranath Tagore, conceptualised by Badal Sarkar, directed by Parnab Mukherjee, starring Sudipta Chakraborty) and numerous other plays.

"Opekkha" from their Bengali album "Proloyer Shomoye" was part of the original soundtrack of the first Bengali sex comedy movie "Aamra" (produced by Venkatesh films, directed by Mainak Bhaumik, cast - Parambrata Chatterjee, Jisshu Sengupta, Rudranil Ghosh, Nilanjana, Ananya Chatterjee, Kaushik Ghosh, Rajatava Datta, Momo).
Strictly Ballroom is a 1992 Australian romantic comedy film directed and co-written by Baz Luhrmann.
The film, Luhrmann's début, is the first in his "The Red Curtain Trilogy" of theatre-motif-related films; it was followed by "Romeo + Juliet" and "Moulin Rouge!

The Miss Utah Teen USA competition is the pageant that selects the representative for the state of Utah in the Miss Teen USA pageant.From 2006, the pageant will be produced by Red Curtain Productions, which includes a number of former Miss Utah Teen USAs and Miss Utah USAs.
Nina Ananiashvili (born: "Nino Ananiashvili", Georgian: ნინო ანანიაშვილი ; born March 28, 1963) is a Georgian ballerina and artistic director of the State Ballet of Georgia.
She has been described by the Daily Telegraph as one of the twelve greatest ballerinas of all time, and in 2002 was named Best Ballerina of the Year by the US "Dance Magazine".
Ananiashvili has been a prominent fixture of the Soviet, Russian and Georgian ballet scene for decades.

Shortly before the fall of the Iron Curtain, in 1987 the New York Magazine critic praised Nina Ananiashvili as "the best thing about the Bolshoi's Giselle whether she appeared in the title role or as the queen of the wilis."
In 2014, a film of her 1991 performance in Giselle with the Bolshoi ballet was released.
Kambuzia Partovi (also spelt Kambozia Partovi, born 11 November 1955 in Rasht) (Persian: کامبوزیا پرتوی‎ ‎ ) is an Iranian film director and scriptwriter.
He was born in Rasht, Iran on the Caspian Sea.
After studying theater arts in school he wrote mainly scripts for TV series.
In 1988 he made his feature film debut with "Golnar".
His 2007 film "Café Transit" which got the special mention at Mar del Plata film festival was selected by Iran as its candidate for the Academy Award for Best Foreign Language Film.
He has also written screenplays for other directors, most notably Jafar Panahi's "The Circle".
Partovi trained and supported many Iranian artists and film makers, most notably Bahman Ghobadi.
In 2013 he acted in and co-directed "Closed Curtain" with Panahi.
Herons Creek is a small township on the North Coast of New South Wales, Australia co-ordinates 31°35′S 152°44′E.
The population of Herons Creek is 312 (2011).
The township is about 291 kilometres (181 miles) north of Sydney.
The North Coast railway line passes through, and a station existed at the site between 1917 and 1974.
Film director Baz Luhrmann was raised in the town.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Role: This agent summarizes first-hop evidence and exposes the bridge entity, any missing fact(s), ambiguity, and concrete next-hop retrieval directions. Do not attempt to produce the final answer—the summary should tell the next agent exactly what to fetch to finish the multi-hop chain.

Output requirements (single `summary` value only):
- Start the summary with the header: First-hop findings:
- Include these labeled parts (use a short bullet list or 2–4 concise sentences; keep overall output brief):
  1) Evidence found: For each person/entity named in the question, list which passage(s) mention matching candidate pages/titles and quote any explicit fact tokens found there (names, birth years, publication years, short parenthetical notes). Keep quoted fragments <=15 words and cite the passage title/label for each fact.
  2) Ambiguities: If a name yields multiple candidate pages or variants in the passages, explicitly enumerate those candidate titles/labels as they appear; if an expected page/variant is missing, note that too.
  3) Bridge entity / Missing fact: Explicitly name the bridge entity exactly as it appears in the passages (the person, work, or organization that links to the next hop) and state the precise missing fact needed to answer the question (e.g., a birth date, founding year, canonical series ordering).
  4) Retrieval direction: Provide 2–4 concrete, prioritized next-hop targets (specific page titles or disambiguation pages to fetch, and/or exact sections to check such as lede/infobox/Career/History). Include short example search keywords or one-line instructions (e.g., "open '<Name>' page and check lede/infobox and 'Career' section for 'Italian champion' year").
- End with a brief explicit note that the final answer is deferred until those pages are retrieved.

Hard constraints (do not violate):
- Do not provide a final comparison or answer here. Do not guess or invent facts not present in the provided passages or introduce outside knowledge.
- Do not assume which candidate is intended when multiple candidates exist.
- Avoid long verbatim copying of passages (>15 words).
- Preserve concise factual phrasing and verbatim tokens that appear in the passages (names, dates, page titles) when reporting evidence.

Keep the summary concise and focused—just the first-hop findings needed to guide the next retrieval hop.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Output contract:
- Produce exactly one field named `query` whose value is a single short retrieval query string (one line, no surrounding explanation or extra fields).
- Do not answer, summarize, or explain anything in `query`. Do not include fallback instructions or user-facing requests for more info in this field.

Construction rules (apply the most specific rule that fits the inputs):
1) Linking two named works: If `question` or `summary_1` names two or more specific works (songs, tracks, titles) and the missing evidence is the bridge between them, emit a concise interrogative that names both works and requests the linking evidence (artist / album / recording). Keep it short (about ≤ 12 words). Example form: "Which artist or album recorded both 'Work A' and 'Work B'?"
2) Missing album artist/genre: If `summary_1` indicates the album title is present but the artist or genre is unknown, emit a compact search string of the form: <AlbumTitle> album artist genre
   - No punctuation, no question phrasing, no extra words. Example: "Levitate album artist genre"
3) Sports season-year lookups: If the `question` contains a specific season-year token and the missing bridge is a league-season page, emit a short query that includes the year token, a league identifier (e.g., "NHL" or "National Hockey League"), and a cue such as "regular season" or "season count". Keep it concise (≤ ~10 tokens).
4) Default: Otherwise, produce one concise search-style query (≤ ~12 words) that uses visible tokens from `question` and `summary_1` targeted at retrieving the missing supporting evidence (artist, album, recording, genre, season page, etc.).

General guidance:
- Keep queries compact; avoid lists, long page titles, or copying large spans from hidden targets.
- Do not invent facts or candidate answers; use only visible tokens or neutral retrieval cues.
- Prefer a short search string; use an interrogative only for the two-works linking case in rule 1.
- The goal is to maximize recovery of the missing bridge evidence for the downstream retriever.

Return only the single `query` field.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Primary function: integrate the newly retrieved `passages` (second-hop evidence) with the provided `context` (first-hop) so the output is a grounded post-retrieval state that preserves the facts the final-answer generator needs. Resolve the earlier bridge when the supplied passages support doing so; retain uncertainty only when evidence is insufficient.

Behavior rules (minimal, general):
1) Preserve concise-answer behavior. If `context` already contains a concise direct answer (a short sentence or phrase), keep that text exactly as the first sentence of `summary`, unless it contradicts the `passages`.
2) When `passages` supply additional, directly supported facts about entities named in the `question` or `context`, append up to one brief supporting sentence per distinct named entity (keep total `summary` to 1–3 sentences, preferably 1–2). Each supporting sentence should: start with the entity's full name as shown in the passages, include a short paraphrased attribution drawn only from the passages (no more than ~10–12 words) such as nationality, a notable achievement, a birth date, or a current affiliation, and avoid inventing or inferring facts not present in the inputs.
3) When an object/entity page (for example, a car page, group page, or similar) lists associated people and a separate passage provides a biography for one of those listed people, produce a 1–2 sentence integrated summary: (a) first sentence: name the people as listed on the object page (e.g., "driven by X and Y" or "member of Group Z"); (b) optional second short clause/sentence: paraphrase key biographical facts from the person's passage (only facts present there — e.g., nationality, notable wins/awards, birth date, current team/affiliation). Keep wording compact and factual.
4) Ground everything in the provided `passages` and `context`. Do not add new claims, dates, roles, or affiliations not explicitly supported. Avoid long verbatim quotations from the passages; you may paraphrase short phrases (≤10 words) only if they appear in the sources, and you may copy short strings such as names or dates exactly as shown.
5) If the passages do not provide enough evidence to resolve the bridge, state that the passages do not specify the requested fact while still listing any supported names or facts. Do not guess or fabricate missing information.
6) Output contract: return only the field `summary` containing 1–3 concise sentences (preferably 1–2) that integrate the first-hop `context` and second-hop `passages` according to the rules above. Do not add commentary, answer-labels, or extraneous text.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2`, produce the field `answer`.

Instructions:
- Use only the visible summaries (summary_1 and summary_2) together with the question as your evidence. Do not introduce external facts, sources, or claims not present in those summaries.
- Treat summary_2 (the later, integrated post-retrieval state) as primary evidence while still considering summary_1. Do not assume the final state is unresolved simply because summary_1 describes an earlier unresolved bridge; rely on the combined visible summaries to reach a conclusion.
- If summary_1 and summary_2 both clearly and unambiguously support the same binary (yes/no) conclusion to the question, then the value of the `answer` field must be exactly one lowercase token: either "yes" (if both summaries affirm the fact) or "no" (if both summaries deny the fact). The `answer` field must contain only that single token and nothing else (no punctuation, no capitalization, no explanation, no additional words).
- If the summaries conflict, are ambiguous, or do not together provide a clear binary yes/no verdict, produce a concise answer string in the `answer` field that best reflects what the summaries support. This concise answer may be a short phrase or sentence, but must still be supported solely by the summaries and must avoid adding new facts.
- Always minimize verbosity: prefer the single-token binary response when the summaries unambiguously agree; otherwise keep the answer brief and directly tied to the visible summaries.

Produce only the `answer` field as output.

response.hop1_query: Which town was the director of the "Red Curtain Trilogy" born in?
hop1_docs: Red Curtain Trilogy | The Red Curtain Trilogy is the title given to the first three films directed by Baz Luhrmann:

Baz Luhrmann | Mark Anthony "Baz" Luhrmann ( ; born 17 September 1962) is an Australian film director, screenwriter and producer best known for "Red Curtain Trilogy", comprising his romantic comedy film "Strictly Ballroom" (1992), the romantic drama "Romeo + Juliet" (1996), and the pastiche-jukebox musical "Moulin Rouge! " (2001).  His 2008 film "Australia" is an epic historical romantic drama film starring Hugh Jackman and Nicole Kidman.  His 2013 drama "The Great Gatsby", based on F. Scott Fitzgerald's 1925 novel of the same name, stars Leonardo DiCaprio (whom he also used in "Romeo + Juliet") and Tobey Maguire.

Strictly Ballroom | Strictly Ballroom is a 1992 Australian romantic comedy film directed and co-written by Baz Luhrmann.  The film, Luhrmann's début, is the first in his "The Red Curtain Trilogy" of theatre-motif-related films; it was followed by "Romeo + Juliet" and "Moulin Rouge! ".

The Curtain (essay) | The Curtain is a seven-part essay by Milan Kundera, along with The Art of the Novel and Testaments Betrayed composing a type of trilogy of book-length essays on the European novel.

Three Colours: Red | Three Colors: Red (French: Trois couleurs: Rouge ) is a 1994 romantic mystery film co-written, produced and directed by Polish filmmaker Krzysztof Kieślowski.  It is the final installment of "The Three Colors Trilogy", which examines the French Revolutionary ideals; it is preceded by "" and "".  Kieślowski had announced that this would be his final film, which proved true with the director's sudden death in 1996.  "Red" is about fraternity, which it examines by showing characters whose lives gradually become closely interconnected, with bonds forming between two characters who appear to have little in common.
Curtains (Red Flag song) | Curtains is a song by the American synthpop band Red Flag.  It was released as a single in 2000.  It was also included as disc V06.0 in the Megablack Box Set.

Kōhaku maku | A kōhaku maku (紅白幕 , literally red and white curtain ) is a type of decorative fabric panel used on various occasions in Japan.  The fabric takes its name from its design of vertical red and white stripes, which is known as "kōhaku".  "Kōhaku maku" panels are hung from a red and white striped rope.
response.summary_1:
First-hop findings:
1) Evidence found:
- Baz Luhrmann: passage [1] ("first three films directed by Baz Luhrmann") and passage [2] (name token "Mark Anthony \"Baz\" Luhrmann" and birth date token "born 17 September 1962") — cite: [1] Red Curtain Trilogy; [2] Baz Luhrmann.
- Films in trilogy: passage [2] lists "Strictly Ballroom (1992), Romeo + Juliet (1996), Moulin Rouge! (2001)" (as the trilogy films) — cite: [2] Baz Luhrmann.

2) Ambiguities:
- The passages show one clear candidate: "Baz Luhrmann" (also given as "Mark Anthony 'Baz' Luhrmann"). No alternate director candidates or disambiguation pages for the director appear in the supplied passages.

3) Bridge entity / Missing fact:
- Bridge entity (as in the passages): "Baz Luhrmann".
- Missing fact needed: the town (place) of birth for Baz Luhrmann (the precise town name is not present in the supplied passages).

4) Retrieval direction (prioritized):
- 1) Open the "Baz Luhrmann" page (lede/infobox) and check the infobox 'Born' and the lede line for birthplace. Example search: "Baz Luhrmann birthplace" or open page and read lede/infobox.
- 2) On the same page, check the "Early life" or "Biography" section for birthplace details (town, state).
- 3) If needed, fetch authoritative biography pages (e.g., BFI, official site) or the "Mark Anthony 'Baz' Luhrmann" redirect/disambiguation to confirm the town. Example search: "Baz Luhrmann Early life born in [town]".
Final answer is deferred until those pages/sections are retrieved.
hop2_docs: The Great Gatsby: Music from Baz Luhrmann's Film | The Great Gatsby: Music from Baz Luhrmann's Film (also known as Music from Baz Luhrmann's Film The Great Gatsby) is the soundtrack album to the 2013 film "The Great Gatsby", an adaptation of F. Scott Fitzgerald's novel of the same name.  Interscope Records released it on May 6, 2013.  The album was produced by Baz Luhrmann and Anton Monsted, with Jay-Z serving as the album's executive producer.  The soundtrack comprises fourteen songs, including new material and cover versions performed by various artists.  It contains a mixture of genre, including hip hop, jazz, and alternative music.  Luhrmann specifically selected these styles of music to better immerse the audience into the story of "The Great Gatsby".
Moulin Rouge! Music from Baz Luhrmann's Film, Vol. 2 | Moulin Rouge!  Music from Baz Luhrmann's Film, Vol.  2

Moulin Rouge! Music from Baz Luhrmann's Film | Moulin Rouge!  Music from Baz Luhrmann's Film is a soundtrack album to Baz Luhrmann's 2001 film "Moulin Rouge! ".  It was released on May 8, 2001.  The album features most of the songs featured in the film.  However, some of the songs are alternate versions and there are two or three major songs that are left off.  The original film versions and extra songs were featured on the second soundtrack.

Strictly Ballroom (musical) | Strictly Ballroom the Musical is a musical theatre adaptation of the 1992 film "Strictly Ballroom".  It is credited as being created by Baz Luhrmann with book by Baz Luhrmann and Craig Pearce adapted by Terry Johnson.  The score consists of existing music and songs, and new musical numbers by David Foster, Sia Furler, Isaac Hasson, Bernie Herms, Baz Lurhmann, MoZella, Neff-U, Craig Pearce, Eddie Perfect, Linda Thompson and Elliot Wheeler.

Something for Everybody (Baz Luhrmann album) | Something for Everybody is an album released by Baz Luhrmann in 1998.  It contains new ambient versions (either newly recorded or remixed) of music from his films and plays, including hits from "William Shakespeare's Romeo + Juliet", "Strictly Ballroom" and "La bohème".  The most popular single from the album is the spoken word song "Everybody's Free (To Wear Sunscreen)".

Strictly Ballroom | Strictly Ballroom is a 1992 Australian romantic comedy film directed and co-written by Baz Luhrmann.  The film, Luhrmann's début, is the first in his "The Red Curtain Trilogy" of theatre-motif-related films; it was followed by "Romeo + Juliet" and "Moulin Rouge! ".

Baz Luhrmann | Mark Anthony "Baz" Luhrmann ( ; born 17 September 1962) is an Australian film director, screenwriter and producer best known for "Red Curtain Trilogy", comprising his romantic comedy film "Strictly Ballroom" (1992), the romantic drama "Romeo + Juliet" (1996), and the pastiche-jukebox musical "Moulin Rouge! " (2001).  His 2008 film "Australia" is an epic historical romantic drama film starring Hugh Jackman and Nicole Kidman.  His 2013 drama "The Great Gatsby", based on F. Scott Fitzgerald's 1925 novel of the same name, stars Leonardo DiCaprio (whom he also used in "Romeo + Juliet") and Tobey Maguire.
